question: Q5: Staying Current Without Tool Fatigue

sections.prompt: Raise your hand if you already feel there are too many AI tools to follow.
sections.hint: Examples: IDE assistants, chatbots, design helpers, CLI agents, plugins.
no_hands: Do you think 'too many tools' is a myth—more choice is better?
If you had to pick one, what would you look for—accuracy, speed, or integration?
Where do you draw the line between exploring and ignoring?
hands_up: How many tools do you actively use today?
Which ones became daily habits, and which ones faded fast?
How do you decide when to uninstall or drop a tool?
Any strategies that kept your workflow clean and not bloated?
follow_ups: Anyone run into conflicts from juggling multiple assistants?
Who has a trick to test new tools quickly without wasting weeks?

sections.prompt: Raise your hand if you’ve ever tried a tool that sounded amazing but didn’t deliver.
sections.hint: Examples: flashy launches, big claims, weak results.
no_hands: How do you spot hype before spending time on a tool?
What would convince you a tool is worth your attention?
Is it safer to wait for peers to test first?
hands_up: Which tools looked great on paper but failed in real Flutter work?
What early warning signs helped you realize it wasn’t worth it?
Has any hyped tool actually lived up to the promise?
What’s your checklist before keeping a tool long-term?
follow_ups: Does anyone rely on benchmarks, blogs, or peer reviews before adopting?
Who has a story of hype turning into a keeper after updates?

sections.prompt: Raise your hand if your team has a rule about how many tools to explore at once.
sections.hint: Examples: balance between stable workflow and trying new assistants.
no_hands: What risks do you see if you never explore new tools?
What risks if you explore too much?
How could you keep curiosity without chaos?
hands_up: How does your team decide when to freeze on a set of tools?
What’s the balance—e.g., stable base tools + one experimental slot?
Has this balance changed over the past year?
Any examples where too much experimentation slowed your velocity?
follow_ups: Who uses trial periods for new tools before wider adoption?
Anyone rotate 'tool scouts' in the team to test on behalf of others?

sections.prompt: Raise your hand if you rely on community signals before picking a tool.
sections.hint: Examples: blogs, podcasts, conference talks, GitHub stars, peer chats.
no_hands: Do you trust community hype cycles, or prefer to evaluate yourself?
Which sources would you trust most for Flutter + AI news?
What signal tells you to ignore a tool completely?
hands_up: Which communities or voices helped you choose well?
What’s the most reliable signal that a tool is real value, not just hype?
Any peers or networks that became your 'filters'?
Has a conference or meetup ever changed your tool choices?
follow_ups: Who has a Slack/Discord/Reddit thread that’s their go-to radar?
Anyone here tried tools only because of Flutter community buzz?

wrap_up: If you had to bet on one AI tool or approach that will last 5 years, what would it be?
What’s one sign you now watch for to avoid tool fatigue?
If you could give one practical tip to peers on keeping sane while exploring AI tools, what would it be?
